summary: DHH argues that bugs are an inevitable and normal byproduct of software development, and that users' emotional reactions to encountering them are disproportionate and counterproductive. He contends that the only way to achieve near-perfect software quality is to write less software, which is commercially unviable. Drawing on Brooks's law, he dismisses the idea that more developers can simply fix all bugs. He frames bug triage as rational prioritization, arguing that the value of software depends far more on the problems it solves than on its bug count. The post calls for realistic expectations and sympathy toward developers rather than outrage.
key_points: Bugs are an inevitable byproduct of writing software; complete eradication is impossible regardless of team size or methodology
The value of software depends far more on the problem it solves than the quality by which it does so — useful buggy software beats useless bug-free software
Bug prioritization through triage (users affected × criticality) is rational and necessary, not a sign of incompetence
Users suffer from myopia when they demand their specific bug be treated as a drop-everything emergency
Emotionally charged responses — demeaning developers, questioning professionalism — make everyone worse off including users
money_quotes: Disappointment occurs when expectations don't match reality. And our expectations for software quality are profoundly unrealistic.
The only reliable, widely used way to ensure impeccable software quality is to write less software that does less stuff, and then spend eons honing that tiny lot.

Bugs are an inevitable byproduct of writing software. Sure, there are all sorts of techniques and potions that promise to decrease how many of the damn critters run about, but only the comically hyperbole pretends that complete eradication is possible.
Useless software can be entirely bug free, yet remain entirely useless. Useful software can be ridden with bugs, yet remain highly valuable.
The value of software depends far more upon the problem it solves than the quality by which it does so.
It's really the pinnacle of myopia when we as users demand and demean software makers to fix our pet bug, just because we hit it.
Marvel at the miracle it is that anything as complex as a modern piece of software works at all!
key_insight: Software bugs are normal and inevitable; the rational response is disciplined triage based on impact, not emotional outrage demanding every bug be treated as an emergency.
